data.title: Stocks to watch on Wednesday after hours: BRX, GBX, CMCO
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes ended lower on Wednesday, with the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) declining due to technology stocks suffering ongoing volatility.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) closed -0.2%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq (COMP:IND) ended -0.7%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) finished flat after having reached a new all-time high mid-session.

Now, here are the three stocks to watch on Wednesday after hours:

  • Brixmor Property Group (BRX) said it acquired four shopping centers for $164.3M as part of its investment activity update for the three and six months ended June 30.
  • Columbus McKinnon (CMCO) appointed John R. Linker as executive vice president of Finance and chief financial officer.
  • Greenbrier (GBX) shares slipped in extended trading hours as its quarterly revenue fell short of the average analyst estimate. Q3 earnings matched the consensus.

data.title: Markets are ‘in waiting more’ as technology stocks struggle, indexes end lower – analyst
data.description:
The stock markets delivered a “humdrum performance” on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) closing down 0.2% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) essentially flat, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) dipped 0.66% as semiconductor companies pulled back.

"It seems as though the market is largely in waiting mode as it seeks additional data for what the future might hold," said Seeking Alpha analyst Daniel Jones. "And, during that time, it is de-risking some of the most expensive companies out there."

The analyst painted a cautious picture of economic conditions, attributing deterioration to inflationary pressures linked to tariffs and the war in Iran.

"If I had to guess, I would say that, before the year is out, we will enter into a stagflationary recession," he said. "And that certainly would not bode well for market participants."

Adding to market uncertainty, new Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh declined to offer forward guidance at the European Central Bank Forum on Central Banking.

"We're going to chart a new course," Warsh said, departing from his predecessor's practice of signaling policy direction.

The Fed chair acknowledged inflation remains too high while emphasizing the central bank's commitment. "We recommit to delivering price stability," he said, adding that anyone expecting the Fed to tolerate above-target inflation "will be disappointed."

The GDPNow model currently projects Q2 2026 real GDP growth at just 1.2%.

Warsh noted that labor markets remain steady and demand solid, though he stressed that getting monetary policy right is "the most important thing we can do."

data.title: Two BlackRock iShares ETFs selected as investment options in Trump Accounts
data.description:
The U.S. Treasury will offer two BlackRock (BLK) iShares ETFs among investment options in Trump Accounts (also known as 530A Accounts).

The ETFs are iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) and iShares Core S&P Total U.S. Stock Market ETF (ITOT).

"IVV and ITOT offer simple, low-cost exposure to the broad U.S. market, making them practical building blocks for long-term investors," said Elise Terry, head of U.S. iShares at BlackRock.

The ETFs are expected to become available through the program in the coming months following the official launch of Trump Accounts.

Trump Accounts are designed to increase long-term savings opportunities for children and families.

"By giving younger Americans the opportunity to start investing earlier, Trump Accounts can help millions build long-term financial security, develop a greater stake in the future of the country, and share more directly in the growth and prosperity of the United States," said CEO Larry Fink.

data.title: Lime rolls into the public market with 6.7M share offering
data.description:
Neutron Holdings (LIME), doing business as Lime, the micromobility operator from San Francisco, California, kicked off its first day as a public company with a 4% gain from its $25 initial public offering price.

The stock opened for trade at $27 per share.

After nearly a decade as a privately owned business, Lime, which rents bicycles and scooters, sold more than 6.6M shares, placing its valuation at $1.66B.

After having flirted with an IPO for at least three years, CEO Wayne Ting found the time was right, made more urgent by “substantial doubts it could continue as a going concern,” a May filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission warned. Without the $167M the IPO raised, Lime would need to raise more funds to address as much as $500M in debt coming due at the end of the year (Lime has nearly $1B in total liabilities).

“Having that resilience and patience and belief and optimism that we will get through the toughest moments has really paid dividends over the long run, because there were many days, weeks, months, where I wasn’t sure if Lime was going to make it past the next three months, four months,” CEO Wayne Ting told TechCrunch in an interview Wednesday.  

The fact that Lime has survived the micromobility business is a testament to its ability to raise cash and generate revenue. The bicycle/scooter rental business is riddled with failures given the high capital costs, severe asset damage, vandalism and theft losses, and shifting urban regulations that plague entrants. Lime competitor Bird filed for bankruptcy in just over two years from its November 2021 IPO, and Helbiz (now operating as Micromobility.com) was delisted from the Nasdaq and never returned as a publicly traded company on a major exchange. Lime even swallowed Jump, future-investor Uber’s own e-bike and scooter business.

Although Lime has managed to expand globally, it owes much of its success to Uber’s (UBER) 24% investment and partnership with the ride-hailing giant. Lime bookings made through Uber accounted for more than 14% of the former’s 2025 revenue.

While the IPO may have given Lime a $167M cash infusion, the company now faces a much more difficult ride. Wall Street will be watching whether it can translate longevity into sustainable earnings growth as a public company.

data.title: Franklin Covey GAAP EPS of $0.27 misses by $0.04, revenue of $67.8M misses by $0.53M
data.description:
• Franklin Covey press release (FC): Q3 GAAP EPS of $0.27 misses by $0.04.
  • Revenue of $67.8M (+1.0% Y/Y) misses by $0.53M.
  • Cash and cash equivalents totaled $12.0 million compared with $33.7 million as of May 31, 2025.

  • The Company updates its fiscal 2026 guidance to the following, in constant currency:

    • Total revenue in the range of $260 million to $267 million, versus prior guidance of $265 million to $275 million vs. a consensus of $267.76M.
    • Adjusted EBITDA in the range of $28 million to $31 million, within prior guidance of $28 million to $33 million.

data.title: Inogen appoints Andy Reding as chief operating officer
data.description:
• Inogen (INGN) said on Wednesday it appointed Andy Reding as chief operating officer, effective July 6.
  • It will be a newly created role that will oversee the medical technology company's commercial and operational execution.

data.title: Sen. Warren asks Fed's IG to review Bowan's attendance at BofA event
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) requested that the Federal Reserve's Inspector General review whether the central bank's vice chair for supervision broke any Fed rules by speaking at a private Bank of America (BAC) dinner last month, according to a media report on Wednesday.

Warren, the top Democrat on the Senate Banking Committee, sent a letter to Inspector General Michael Horowitz, the Wall Street Journal reported. She  asked whether Michelle Bowman's “attendance or comments violated any statutes, rules, regulations, policies, or procedures and whether the Fed’s existing framework governing such external events should be strengthened.”

Senators Jack Reed (D-RI) and Chris Van Hollen (D-MD) also signed the letter, the article said. The newspaper had reported that Bowman spoke at an invitation-only dinner on the evening of June 17, a few hours after the Federal Open Market Committee left its policy rate unchanged. The dinner took place within the Fed's blackout period, which spanned from June 6 through June 18, 2026.

In a statement to the WSJ, Bowman said she didn't discuss monetary policy at the dinner and has "consistently complied with all applicable FOMC and ethics rules."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Brixmor Property acquires four shopping centers for $164M
data.description:
Brixmor Property Group (BRX) on Wednesday said it acquired four shopping centers for $164.3M as part of its investment activity update for the three and six months ended June 30.

The acquisitions are Mayfair Shopping Center acquired for $70.0M, Jones Crossing for $46.5M, Vintage Marketplace for $32.7M, and Stanford Station for $15.1M.

"These acquisitions build on our clustering strategy and provide us additional pathways to create value over time through leasing, reinvestment, and densification," said Chief Investment Officer Mark Horgan.

"Notably, the Mayfair Shopping Center transaction was a milestone for the company as we issued OP units to fund an acquisition for the first time in our history, expanding our capital toolkit in a meaningful way," said Horgan.

During the three months ended June 30, the retail REIT disposed two shopping centers for proceeds of ~$15.1M.

data.title: Palantir CEO speaks out against 'tokenmaxxing' being pushed by frontier labs
data.description:
Palantir (PLTR) CEO Alex Karp spoke out against the token pricing model being pushed by frontier labs such as Anthropic (ANTHRO) and OpenAI (OPENAI) during an interview with CNBC on Wednesday.

"I'm not throwing shade at them, but something has gone completely wrong," he told CNBC. "The basic view among enterprises in this country is I'm going to chillax and waste my time with tokens."

Palantir closed with a nearly 8% gain on Wednesday.

The subsidized AI ride appears to be over as some enterprises are finding out the hard way about the rising costs of new models and surging token usage. This is prompting some businesses to lean into open-weight models that are typically cheaper to use.

"Tokenmaxxing hijacks your value orientation and decreases your institutional fortitude and intelligence," Palantir said on X in a nine-point post on the importance of AI sovereignty. "The pursuit of high token usage incentivizes disposable scripts over robust software—with the addictive feeling of false progress. There is a reason why those selling tokens refuse to charge based on value."

Some businesses are also developing their own AI models that are customized to meet the specific needs of the enterprise or agency. Earlier this week, Palantir revealed a new collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA). It uses Nvidia's open-source Nemotron models to help the government develop custom AI systems while reducing the security and proprietary issues associated with closed models.

"Your AI sovereignty dictates your institution's future," Palantir said on X. "Sovereignty is the precondition for choice. Relinquishing sovereignty transfers the future choices of your institution to others, who are likely to exploit it for their gain and your loss."

"What aligns me with Nvidia, and I think is what the technical customers want, which is control over their compute, their models, their data stack and their alpha," Karp said to CNBC. "They want to know they own the means of production. It’s not being transferred to someone else."

data.title: Global-E Online closes on Passport acquisition as it adds to its shipping capabilities
data.description:
Global-E Online Ltd. (GLBE) closed on its $350M acquisition of Passport Global on Wednesday. The deal to pick up the U.S.-based cross-border e-commerce logistics and solutions company is expected to further strengthen Global-E's (GLBE) logistics capabilities, adding advanced standard shipping solutions powered by an efficient, asset-light, multi-carrier network across cross-border, domestic, and last-mile deliveries. 

The capabilities and network from Passport will also enhance Global-e's (GLBE) ability to execute direct injection and consolidated returns, supporting the delivery of scalable and cost-effective logistics solutions. In addition, through the Passport brand, Global-e (GLBE) will provide a non-Merchant of Record (non-MoR) solution to address and provide support for certain merchant segments.

"By bringing Passport’s advanced cross-border logistics infrastructure and asset-light, multi-carrier network together with our end-to-end platform, we can deliver more reliable and cost-effective shipping, faster delivery, and a better post-purchase experience, creating real, tangible value for our merchants and their customers around the world," highlighted Global-e (GLBE) President Nir Debbi.

The acquisition was funded via approximately equal portions of cash on hand and ordinary shares of Global-e (GLBE). An additional contingent consideration of up to $75M is subject to Passport achieving certain financial results.

Passport is expected to generate approximately $100M in revenues in calendar year 2026 with a rapid growth rate, which is expected to slightly exceed that of Global-e (GLBE) for the year. The acquisition is expected to have a neutral to slightly positive effect on Global-E's (GLBE) adjusted EBITDA contribution in the second half of 2026.
